behaviors.description: Only files changed in the pull request are analyzed — unchanged files are not scanned.
behaviors.justification: The failure is that the code analyzes files outside the changed set. This can be caught by mocking the GitHub API/environment to specify a particular set of changed files (e.g., ['src/main.js', 'src/utils.js']), then verifying the linter is invoked exactly for those files and no others. The filtering logic is a pure boundary test—no filesystem or subprocess needed.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the GitHub API (or environment variables) to return a fixed set of changed files. Mock the linter to track all invocations. Verify linter.analyze() is called exactly for the changed files; assert it is never called for other files in the project. Use assertion on call arguments to catch both missing files and extra files.

behaviors.description: Findings are grouped by severity (error, warning, info) in the posted PR comment.
behaviors.justification: The failure is that the posted comment has wrong structure or missing grouping. The comment-building logic can be tested by mocking the linter output (return a mix of error/warning/info findings) and mocking the GitHub PR posting API, then verifying the generated comment text contains proper grouping headers/structure. The formatting logic itself requires no I/O.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the linter to return findings with mixed severity levels (at least one error, warning, and info). Mock GitHub's comment-posting API to capture the posted text. Verify the comment contains distinct sections for each severity, with findings correctly placed in each section. Verify no findings are in the wrong severity group.

behaviors.description: A pull request that touches 200 files completes analysis within the 10-minute Actions timeout.
behaviors.justification: This is a performance boundary. Mocking the linter at Unit level would not reveal real timing behavior. The real failure is that the linter, called 200 times (or on 200 files), exceeds 10 minutes. This requires running the actual linter process on real or realistic test files. A local Integration test (200 test files, real linter execution) can catch gross inefficiency (e.g., O(n²) filtering). System-level testing (GitHub Actions environment) would be more realistic but Integration is the minimum to surface the failure.
behaviors.test_description: Prepare or generate a test corpus of 200 files of varying sizes and complexity (to simulate real PR changes). Run the complete lint pipeline (changed-file detection, linter invocation, comment building) on these files. Measure wall-clock time from start to finish. Assert that total execution time is well below 10 minutes (e.g., target < 6 minutes for 2× safety margin). Run this test repeatedly to catch non-deterministic slowdowns.

behaviors.description: When the linter process crashes on a malformed file, the remaining files are still analyzed and results posted.
behaviors.justification: The failure is that a crash in the linter process causes the entire analysis to fail and no results are posted. The real scenario involves a subprocess crash (exit code != 0, or killed signal). A Unit test that only mocks an exception thrown by the linter function could miss subprocess-specific behavior (e.g., partial output, signal handling). Integration testing (simulate a crash via a test subprocess or mock subprocess exit) more realistically tests the orchestration logic's error recovery. The core failure—'remaining files not analyzed'—can technically be caught at Unit by mocking an exception, but Integration is safer.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test scenario where the linter process crashes/exits non-zero on file N (either via a real linter crash on malformed input, or a mock subprocess that exits with code 1). Verify: (a) the crash is caught and logged, (b) the orchestrator continues processing files N+1 onwards, (c) partial results from successfully analyzed files are still posted to the PR, and (d) the error is reported in the posted comment or in logs.

behaviors.description: Duplicate findings on the same line from overlapping rules are deduplicated in the output.
behaviors.justification: The failure is that duplicate findings are not removed. This is pure deduplication logic with no I/O. Mock the linter to return a list of findings with exact duplicates (same line, same rule, same message) and verify the deduplication function produces exactly the unique set. No filesystem, subprocess, or external API needed.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the linter to return a set of findings including exact duplicates (e.g., two findings for line 5 from 'rule-a'). Pass this to the deduplication logic. Verify the output contains only the unique findings and no duplicates. Test both duplicate (identical) and near-duplicate (same line, different rule) cases to ensure the deduplication criteria are correct.
